---
title: "Hilbert-space Gaussian processes: smoothing with kernels"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
A **Gaussian-process** smooth asks for smoothness a different way: instead of
"penalise the second difference", you say "function values at nearby inputs are
correlated, with the correlation decaying over a **lengthscale** $\rho$" ‚Äî a
kernel. Evaluated at the data, that prior is a random effect with covariance
$K$, so a *fixed-kernel* GP is already an [LME](lme.qmd) (penalty $S=K^{-1}$). The
hard part is **estimating $\rho$**, because changing it changes $K$ and its
eigenvectors. **HSGP** (Hilbert-space approximate GP) fixes that: expand the GP in
a fixed basis of **Laplace eigenfunctions** (sines), into which the kernel enters
*only* as a **diagonal reweighting** of the columns by the kernel's *spectral
density*. Then the basis never moves, $\rho$ becomes a cheap parameter to
estimate, the amplitude is the [Fellner‚ÄìSchall](fellner-schall.qmd) smoothing
parameter, and a GP is one more [penalty block](gamm.qmd) you hand to
`gam_fit` ‚Äî or, with the lengthscale estimated for you, to `gp_fit`.
:::

Rung 6 ‚Äî the top of [the ladder](index.qmd). It builds on the
[GAM](gam.qmd)/[LME](lme.qmd) equivalence and the spectral idea from the
[LME chapter](lme.qmd#sec-fastlmm).

::: {.callout-note title="Where this lives"}
The GP rung ships with the `stats-gp` work (`nitrix.stats.gp` ‚Äî  `gp_fit`,
`gp_predict`, `GPResult`; `nitrix.stats.hgp` ‚Äî `hgp_fit` for hierarchical GPs;
`hsgp_basis` / `hsgp_basis_nd` in `nitrix.stats.basis`; the lengthscale priors in
`nitrix.stats.priors`). It is being folded into the suite; this chapter describes
that implementation.
:::

## A motivating example {#sec-motivation}

Cortical thickness falls with age, but not on a straight line: it dips in
adolescence, plateaus, then declines. You have thickness at one vertex for $N$
subjects against age $x$, and you want the trajectory $f(x)$ without committing to
a polynomial degree. A [GAM](gam.qmd) would lay down a spline basis and penalise
the wiggliness. A **Gaussian process** answers the same question by stating a
belief about *correlation*: "subjects close in age should have similar fitted
thickness, and how fast that similarity decays with age-gap is a thing I will
**learn** from the data." That decay rate is the lengthscale, and learning it is
the whole story of this chapter. (Multiply the vertex up to the whole cortex and
you want to do this for hundreds of thousands of vertices at once ‚Äî which is why
the cheap construction below matters.)

## Smoothness as correlation

A [GAM](gam.qmd) smooth says "don't be wiggly" through a difference penalty. A
**Gaussian process** says it through a *covariance*: model the unknown function as

$$
f \sim \mathcal{GP}\big(0,\ k(x, x')\big),
$$ {#eq-gp}

where the **kernel** $k$ sets how strongly $f(x)$ and $f(x')$ co-vary. A Mat√©rn or
squared-exponential kernel has two knobs: an **amplitude** (how far $f$ ranges)
and a **lengthscale** $\rho$ (how quickly correlation decays with distance ‚Äî large
$\rho$ = gently varying, small $\rho$ = wiggly). You specify *how points relate*
rather than *which derivative to penalise*.

## A fixed-kernel GP is a mixed model

Evaluate the prior @eq-gp at the observed inputs and it becomes an ordinary
multivariate Gaussian, $f \sim \mathcal{N}(0, K)$ with $K_{ij} = k(x_i, x_j)$.
That is precisely a **random effect** ‚Äî the [LME](lme.qmd) chapter's $b$ ‚Äî only
with covariance $K$ in place of $\sigma_b^2 ZZ^{\top}$. Equivalently it is a
penalty block with $S = K^{-1}$. So if the kernel (and hence $\rho$) is **fixed**,
GP regression is *already in the suite*: it is the LME fit with $ZGZ^{\top}\to K$,
or a GAM smooth whose penalty is $S=K^{-1}$ (the kriging `gp_basis`). Nothing new ‚Äî
this is the [grand equivalence](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence) read one more
time, with the random-effect covariance supplied by a kernel.

## The hard part: estimating the lengthscale

The catch is that we usually do **not** know $\rho$, and we want to learn it
(that was the whole point of the cortical-thickness example). But $K$ depends on
$\rho$ through and through ‚Äî change $\rho$ and the entire matrix, **including its
eigenvectors**, changes. The [FaST-LMM trick](lme.qmd) that made variance
estimation cheap relied on diagonalising a *fixed* structure once; if the
structure moves every step, we are back to an $O(N^3)$ eigendecomposition per
iteration. We need a representation in which the kernel hyperparameters do **not**
touch the basis.

## HSGP: the Laplace-eigenfunction construction {#sec-hsgp}

Here is the idea that makes it cheap, built from scratch.

### The obvious thing, and why we change basis

The obvious thing is to keep working with $K$ and re-factorise it at every
candidate $\rho$. That fails for exactly the reason above: the basis moves. The
cure is to expand $f$ in a basis that is *fixed once and for all*, and let $\rho$
act only on the **coefficients**. Which basis? We want one that is natural for
"smoothness on an interval", and there is a canonical answer.

### The vibrating string: Laplace eigenfunctions

Picture a string pinned at both ends of an interval $[c-L,\,c+L]$. Pluck it and it
rings in **standing waves** ‚Äî the fundamental, then the first harmonic, then the
second ‚Äî each a pure sine that vanishes at the two ends. These standing waves are
the **eigenfunctions of the Laplacian** $-\,d^2/dx^2$ with Dirichlet (pinned-end)
boundary conditions: functions $\phi_j$ for which $-\phi_j'' = \omega_j^2\,\phi_j$.
On the bounded domain there is a *discrete* ladder of them,

$$
\phi_j(x) = \sqrt{\tfrac1L}\,\sin\!\Big(\omega_j\,(x - c + L)\Big),
\qquad \omega_j = \frac{j\pi}{2L},\quad j = 1,2,\dots
$$ {#eq-eigfun}

The integer $j$ counts the half-waves on the interval, $\omega_j$ is the spatial
frequency (eigenvalue $\omega_j^2$), and the $\sqrt{1/L}$ normalises each one.
Two facts make these the right basis. First, **they depend only on the domain,
never on the kernel** ‚Äî no $\rho$ anywhere in @eq-eigfun. Second, **low $j$ is
smooth, high $j$ is wiggly**, by construction: the index *is* a frequency, so a
function built from the first few $\phi_j$ is automatically gentle. This is the
fixed basis `_hsgp_eigen` builds in `gp.py` (it returns $\omega_j$, a per-mode
phase $\omega_j(L-c)$ that folds the centring constant in, and the $\sqrt{1/L}$
amplitude), and `_hsgp_features` evaluates it as $\Phi = [\phi_j(x)]$.
@fig-hsgp-eigfun draws the first few: each is a pure sine with one more half-wave
than the last, and the index $j$ literally counts the wiggles ‚Äî which is why
truncating the expansion at $m$ modes is the same as refusing to represent
anything wigglier than the $m$-th harmonic.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hsgp-eigfun
#| fig-cap: "The first five Laplace‚ÄìDirichlet eigenfunctions œÜ‚±º on the interval [‚àí1, 1] ‚Äî the standing waves of a string pinned at both ends. Each vanishes at the boundary and carries one more half-wave than the last (j counts the wiggles), and none of them depends on the kernel or the lengthscale. This is the fixed basis; the kernel will only choose how heavily to weight each one."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

L = 1.0          # half-width of the domain [-1, 1]
c = 0.0          # centre
x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 400)

def phi(x, j):                                   # eq-eigfun: standing wave j
    omega_j = j * np.pi / (2 * L)
    return np.sqrt(1.0 / L) * np.sin(omega_j * (x - c + L))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
for j in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]:
    ax.plot(x, phi(x, j), lw=2, label=f"j = {j}")
ax.axhline(0, color="0.7", lw=0.8)
ax.set(title="Laplace‚ÄìDirichlet eigenfunctions: a fixed sine basis (no kernel, no œÅ)",
       xlabel="x", ylabel=r"$\phi_j(x)$")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=5, loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

### Where the kernel comes back: the spectral density

We have a basis with no kernel in it. So where does the choice of kernel ‚Äî RBF
vs. Mat√©rn, this lengthscale vs. that one ‚Äî re-enter? Through **how much we trust
each frequency**.

A stationary kernel $k(x,x')=k(x-x')$ has a **spectral density** $S(\omega)$: its
Fourier transform, telling you how the function's "energy" is spread across
frequencies (this is Bochner's theorem ‚Äî a stationary covariance and a
non-negative spectral density are the same information). A smooth kernel puts most
of its mass at low $\omega$; a rough one keeps mass out at high $\omega$. The
reduced-rank insight of Solin & S√§rkk√§ is that on the bounded domain you can
**approximate the kernel by reweighting the fixed eigenfunctions by the spectral
density evaluated at their own frequency**:

$$
k(x,x') \;\approx\; \sum_{j=1}^m S(\omega_j)\,\phi_j(x)\,\phi_j(x').
$$ {#eq-hsgp-kernel}

Read it piece by piece. $\phi_j(x)\phi_j(x')$ is the rank-one outer product of one
standing wave ‚Äî the contribution of frequency $\omega_j$ to the covariance. The
scalar $S(\omega_j)$ is *how much of that frequency the kernel wants*: a number per
eigenfunction. Summing the first $m$ of them rebuilds $k$. @fig-hsgp-approx shows
this series converging as $m$ grows.

Now compare @eq-hsgp-kernel to the eigendecomposition of a covariance matrix,
$K = \sum_j d_j\, u_j u_j^{\top}$. They are the *same shape*: the $\phi_j$ play the
role of fixed eigenvectors $u_j$, and $S(\omega_j)$ plays the role of the
eigenvalue $d_j$ ‚Äî the **prior variance of the $j$-th coefficient**. That is the
punchline. If we write $f(x)=\sum_j \gamma_j\,\phi_j(x)$, then @eq-hsgp-kernel says
the coefficients are independent Gaussians with

$$
\gamma_j \sim \mathcal{N}\big(0,\ S(\omega_j)\big),
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
S \;=\; \operatorname{diag}\!\Big(\tfrac{1}{S(\omega_1)},\dots,\tfrac{1}{S(\omega_m)}\Big),
$$ {#eq-hsgp-penalty}

a **diagonal penalty matrix** $S$ (precision = inverse prior variance, exactly the
penalty-is-precision reading of [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence)). A
Gaussian process is *another penalty block* ‚Äî and a particularly nice one,
because $S$ is diagonal. This is the same "every smoother is a penalty block"
unification the [GAMM chapter](gamm.qmd#sec-unification) builds to; the GP is its
last entry.

### Why one fixed basis works for *any* lengthscale

Here is the property that makes lengthscale estimation cheap. The eigenfunctions
$\phi_j$ in @eq-eigfun do not contain $\rho$. Changing the lengthscale changes only
the **weights** $S(\omega_j)$ in @eq-hsgp-penalty ‚Äî it slides prior variance
between the *same* fixed columns, never moving them. So estimating $\rho$ is
optimising a diagonal reweighting of a matrix we factor once, not re-factorising a
moving kernel. There is **no eigendecomposition in the loop**. Two payoffs:

- **The basis is fixed.** The lengthscale enters as a diagonal reweighting
  $1/S(\omega_j;\rho)$ of a fixed design ‚Äî cheap, and `eigh`-free.
- **It is a drop-in smooth.** Fold the weights into the design,
  $\Psi = \big[\sqrt{S(\omega_j)}\,\phi_j(x)\big]$, and the penalty becomes the
  **identity**: $\Psi$ is then nothing but another [GAM](gam.qmd) basis, and the
  [Fellner‚ÄìSchall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update) smoothing parameter
  $\lambda = 1/\sigma_f^2$ *is* the GP amplitude. This is exactly what
  `hsgp_basis(x, n_basis=m, kernel=..., rho=...)` returns ‚Äî a `SplineBasis` of
  `kind='hsgp'` you hand straight to `gam_fit`, lengthscale fixed.

## The spectral densities, kernel by kernel {#sec-spectral}

`nitrix` supports four stationary kernels; their spectral densities are the real
formulas in `nitrix.linalg.kernel` (`se_spectral_density`,
`matern_spectral_density`, dispatched by `spectral_density`). In 1-D, with
amplitude folded into the smoothing parameter:

- **Squared-exponential / RBF** $k(r)=\exp(-r^2/2\rho^2)$:
  $$
  S_{\mathrm{RBF}}(\omega) \;=\; \sqrt{2\pi}\,\rho\;\exp\!\big(-\tfrac12\rho^2\omega^2\big).
  $$ {#eq-S-rbf}
  A Gaussian in $\omega$ ‚Äî it decays *super-fast*, so an RBF GP is extremely
  smooth (infinitely differentiable). Doubling $\rho$ both narrows the Gaussian
  (concentrating mass at low frequency) and lifts its height.

- **Mat√©rn-$\nu$** with rate $\lambda = \sqrt{2\nu}/\rho$ (matching scikit-learn's
  `Matern(length_scale=rho)`), for $\nu\in\{\tfrac12,\tfrac32,\tfrac52\}$:
  $$
  S_{1/2}(\omega)=\frac{2\lambda}{\lambda^2+\omega^2},\quad
  S_{3/2}(\omega)=\frac{4\lambda^3}{(\lambda^2+\omega^2)^2},\quad
  S_{5/2}(\omega)=\frac{16}{3}\,\frac{\lambda^5}{(\lambda^2+\omega^2)^3}.
  $$ {#eq-S-matern}
  These decay only *polynomially* in $\omega$ ‚Äî a **heavy tail** that keeps some
  mass at high frequency, so a Mat√©rn GP admits rougher functions than an RBF one
  ($\nu=\tfrac12$ is the non-differentiable exponential kernel; $\nu=\tfrac52$ is
  twice-differentiable, the sensible default for trajectories). The smoothness
  index $\nu$ is fixed by your choice of kernel; $\rho$ slides the cutoff.

The lengthscale does one thing to all of these: **a long $\rho$ concentrates
spectral mass at low frequency**, so @eq-hsgp-penalty upweights the gentle
low-$j$ eigenfunctions and starves the wiggly high-$j$ ones ‚Äî a smooth fit. A short
$\rho$ spreads mass to higher $\omega$ and lets the wiggles back in.
@fig-hsgp-spectral makes this visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hsgp-spectral
#| fig-cap: "Spectral densities S(œâ), normalised to S(0)=1 so the *shape* is what we compare. Left: RBF ‚Äî a Gaussian in œâ that dies fast. Right: Mat√©rn-5/2 ‚Äî a polynomial tail that lingers at high frequency (so Mat√©rn admits rougher functions). In both, the long lengthscale (œÅ=0.5, solid) packs mass at low œâ (smooth); the short lengthscale (œÅ=0.15, dashed) spreads it to high œâ (wiggly)."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1-D closed forms from nitrix.linalg.kernel:
#   RBF       S(w) ‚àù exp(-rho^2 w^2 / 2)
#   Matern52  S(w) ‚àù lam^5 / (lam^2 + w^2)^3,  lam = sqrt(5)/rho
omega = np.linspace(0, 30, 400)

def rbf_S(om, rho):
    s = np.exp(-0.5 * (rho * om) ** 2)
    return s / s[0]

def matern52_S(om, rho):
    lam = np.sqrt(5.0) / rho
    s = lam**5 / (lam**2 + om**2) ** 3
    return s / s[0]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.4, 3.4), sharey=True)
for rho, style in [(0.5, "-"), (0.15, "--")]:
    ax[0].plot(omega, rbf_S(omega, rho), style, lw=2, label=f"œÅ = {rho}")
    ax[1].plot(omega, matern52_S(omega, rho), style, lw=2, label=f"œÅ = {rho}")
ax[0].set(title="RBF spectral density", xlabel="frequency œâ", ylabel="S(œâ) / S(0)")
ax[1].set(title="Mat√©rn-5/2 spectral density", xlabel="frequency œâ")
for a in ax:
    a.legend(fontsize=9)
    a.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
fig.suptitle("Long œÅ ‚Üí mass at low frequency ‚Üí smooth; heavy Mat√©rn tail keeps wiggles", fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## How many eigenfunctions? Approximation quality vs $m$ {#sec-rank}

The sum in @eq-hsgp-kernel is truncated at $m$ terms. Why is a small $m$ enough,
when the exact GP has one "basis function" per data point ($N$ of them)? Because
the spectral density *kills the high-frequency terms for you*: once $\omega_j$ is
large, $S(\omega_j)$ is tiny (@eq-S-rbf, @eq-S-matern), so those eigenfunctions
carry almost no prior variance and contribute almost nothing to the sum. You only
need enough modes to reach the frequencies the kernel still cares about. The rule
of thumb: **a shorter lengthscale needs more modes** (it keeps higher frequencies
alive), which is why `gp_fit` defaults to `rank=20` for a 1-D HSGP and
`hsgp_basis` to `n_basis=20`. @fig-hsgp-approx reconstructs a kernel column and
watches the truncation converge.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hsgp-approx
#| fig-cap: "Reconstructing a Mat√©rn-5/2 kernel column k(x, 0) by the HSGP series ‚àë‚±º S(œâ‚±º) œÜ‚±º(x) œÜ‚±º(0) for m = 4, 8, 16, 32 eigenfunctions (normalised to peak 1). With only m=4 the curve is visibly too wiggly at the tails; by m=16 it sits on the exact kernel (dashed). The spectral density downweights the high-j modes, so a modest m ‚â™ N suffices."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

L_dom = 1.0           # domain [-1, 1]
c = 0.0
x = np.linspace(-1, 1, 300)
x0 = 0.0              # reconstruct the column k(x, 0)
rho = 0.25

def phi(x, j):                                   # Laplace eigenfunctions (eq-eigfun)
    omega_j = j * np.pi / (2 * L_dom)
    return np.sqrt(1.0 / L_dom) * np.sin(omega_j * (x - c + L_dom))

def S(j, rho):                                   # Matern-5/2 spectral density at œâ_j
    omega_j = j * np.pi / (2 * L_dom)
    lam = np.sqrt(5.0) / rho
    return (16.0 / 3.0) * lam**5 / (lam**2 + omega_j**2) ** 3

def k_true(x, x0, rho):                          # exact Matern-5/2 covariance
    a = np.sqrt(5.0) * np.abs(x - x0) / rho
    return (1.0 + a + a * a / 3.0) * np.exp(-a)

def k_approx(x, x0, rho, m):                     # the truncated HSGP series (eq-hsgp-kernel)
    j = np.arange(1, m + 1)
    return (S(j, rho)[None, :] * phi(x[:, None], j[None, :])
            * phi(np.array([x0]), j)[None, :]).sum(axis=1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.6))
kt = k_true(x, x0, rho); kt = kt / kt.max()
ax.plot(x, kt, "k--", lw=2, label="exact Mat√©rn-5/2")
for m in [4, 8, 16, 32]:
    ka = k_approx(x, x0, rho, m); ka = ka / ka.max()
    ax.plot(x, ka, lw=1.6, label=f"m = {m}")
ax.set(title="HSGP reconstruction of a kernel column k(x, 0) as m grows",
       xlabel="x", ylabel="k(x, 0) (normalised)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=2)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## The kernel as a reweighting, by hand

A minimal HSGP ‚Äî fixed sine basis, Mat√©rn-5/2 spectral weights, an identity-ridge
solve ‚Äî in pure NumPy. Watch the lengthscale change the *weights on a fixed
design*, not the design.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hsgp
#| fig-cap: "HSGP with a fixed sine basis. The lengthscale œÅ only reweights the columns (via the Mat√©rn-5/2 spectral density); a short œÅ keeps high-frequency sines and fits wiggles, a long œÅ damps them and fits a gentle curve."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n = 200
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
f_true = np.sin(2*np.pi*x) + 0.3*x
y = f_true + rng.normal(0, 0.35, n)

m = 40                                            # retained eigenfunctions
c = 0.5*(x.min()+x.max()); L = 1.5*0.5*(x.max()-x.min())
j = np.arange(1, m+1)
omega = j*np.pi/(2*L)                             # sqrt(lambda_j), the eigenfreqs
phi = np.sqrt(1/L)*np.sin(np.outer(x - c + L, omega))   # fixed sine basis (n√óm)

def matern52_S(om, rho):                          # spectral density weights (shape only)
    nu = 2.5
    return (2*nu/rho**2 + om**2)**(-(nu + 0.5))

def hsgp_fit(rho, tau=1e-3):
    s = matern52_S(omega, rho)
    Psi = phi * np.sqrt(s)[None, :]               # kernel enters ONLY here, as weights
    beta = np.linalg.solve(Psi.T@Psi + tau*np.eye(m), Psi.T@y)
    return Psi @ beta

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=8, alpha=0.25, color="0.5", label="data")
ax.plot(x, f_true, "k--", lw=1.5, label="truth")
for rho, name in [(0.03, "short œÅ (wiggly)"), (0.5, "long œÅ (smooth)")]:
    ax.plot(x, hsgp_fit(rho), lw=2, label=name)
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=2); ax.set(title="HSGP: lengthscale reweights a fixed sine basis")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The basis `phi` is built once; only `sqrt(s)` changes with $\rho$. That is the
whole reason `gp_fit` can estimate a lengthscale cheaply ‚Äî it is optimising a
diagonal reweighting of a fixed matrix, never re-factorising a moving kernel.

## Estimating the lengthscale: `gp_fit` and `GPResult` {#sec-gpfit}

`hsgp_basis` fixes $\rho$ and rides `gam_fit`. The standalone `gp_fit` closes the
loop and **estimates** $\rho$ too. The model it fits per element (voxel/vertex)
is exactly the cortical-thickness one:

$$
y_v = T\alpha_v + f_v(x) + e_v,\qquad
f_v \sim \mathcal{GP}(0,\sigma_f^2 K_\rho),\qquad
e_v \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_e^2),
$$

with `T` the optional unpenalised `parametric` design (intercept plus any linear
covariates). The clever part is the **profiling**: for a candidate $\rho$ the model
is a Gaussian penalised regression with a single smoothing parameter
$\lambda = \sigma_e^2/\sigma_f^2$ ‚Äî the GAM identity again, where the
Fellner‚ÄìSchall parameter *is* the inverse GP amplitude. So `gp_fit` nests two
loops:

- **Inner (fixed $\rho$):** the generalized
  [Fellner‚ÄìSchall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update) step (Wood & Fasiolo 2017)
  picks $\lambda$ ‚Äî the same `gam_fit` machinery, here with one diagonal penalty
  $\operatorname{diag}(1/S(\omega_j;\rho))$ on the smooth block (`_fs_lambda`,
  `_penalty_diag`).
- **Outer (select $\rho$):** a 1-D search over $\log\rho$ of the **pooled REML
  marginal likelihood** $\sum_v \ell_R(\rho,\lambda_v)$ on a fixed log-spaced grid
  (`n_rho=24` points, default range $(0.05,2.0)\times$ data-range) refined by a
  parabola (`_parabolic_argmin`). The lengthscale $\rho$ is **shared across
  elements** (one eigenbasis, one smoothness), while the amplitude $\sigma_f^2$
  and noise $\sigma_e^2$ are **per element** ‚Äî the natural mass-univariate
  factoring.

With $\sigma_e^2$ profiled out, the per-element restricted negative log-likelihood
that `_reml_nll` evaluates is (up to a constant in $N$ and the unpenalised count
$M_0$ only)

$$
-2\,\ell_R = (N - M_0)\log D_p + \log|H| - \log|S_\lambda|_{+},
$$ {#eq-reml-nll}

with $H = X^{\top}X + S_\lambda$ the penalised Hessian, $D_p = y^{\top}y -
\beta^{\top}X^{\top}y$ the penalised residual sum of squares, $S_\lambda =
\lambda\,\operatorname{diag}(0,\dots,0,1/S(\omega_1),\dots)$ (zeros on the fixed
columns), and $\log|S_\lambda|_{+} = m\log\lambda - \sum_j \log S(\omega_j)$ its
log-pseudo-determinant. This is the [(RE)ML objective](lme.qmd#sec-reml) of the
LME chapter, now profiled to a function of $\rho$ alone. Every solve goes through
the dense `small_inv_logdet` (the suite's batched
[Cholesky-with-log-determinant](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky)) ‚Äî no per-element
$(N\times N)$ GP covariance is ever materialised; peak memory is $O(V(m+q)^2)$.

`gp_fit` returns a **`GPResult`** with: `coef` $(V,p)$ over `[fixed | smooth]`;
`cov_unscaled` $(V,p,p)$ the Bayesian covariance $(X^{\top}X+S_\lambda)^{-1}$;
`theta` $(V,3)$ the per-element $[\log\sigma_f^2,\log\sigma_e^2,\log\rho]$ (the
$\rho$ column constant, since it is shared); `log_mlik`, `edf` (the total effective
degrees of freedom $\operatorname{tr}((X^{\top}X+S_\lambda)^{-1}X^{\top}X)$ ‚Äî see
the [GAM EDF discussion](gam.qmd#sec-edf)), and `dispersion`. `gp_predict` then
re-evaluates the (fixed) eigenfunctions at new inputs and returns the posterior
mean $X_{\text{new}}\beta$ and standard deviation
$\sqrt{\sigma_e^2\operatorname{diag}(X_{\text{new}}VX_{\text{new}}^{\top})}$.

Two engines sit behind the same API. The default `engine='hsgp'` is everything
above. The alternative `engine='exact'` does a one-off host eigendecomposition of
the actual kernel Gram per $\rho$ (`_exact_eigen`), building the Karhunen‚ÄìLo√®ve
features $\Phi = U\Lambda^{1/2}$; it is the full-rank GP when `rank == N` and the
eigen-truncated (Nystr√∂m) approximation otherwise ‚Äî equivalent to `lme.reml_fit`
by the penalty‚Üîvariance-component identity, useful as a ground-truth check on the
HSGP approximation. You can also regularise $\rho$ with a prior via `map_rho=`
(see ¬ß9.1).

### Lengthscale priors (`map_rho`) {#sec-priors}

A lengthscale can be weakly identified ‚Äî short series, sparse coverage ‚Äî and pure
REML can then chase $\rho$ toward noise-fitting (tiny $\rho$) or toward
near-linear (huge $\rho$). The `priors` module supplies penalised-complexity-style
regularisers, each a callable $\rho \mapsto -\log p(\rho)$ that `gp_fit` *adds* to
the pooled REML objective in @eq-reml-nll, turning the selected $\rho$ into a
**maximum-a-posteriori** value:

- `invgamma_prior(a, b)`: $-\log p = (a+1)\log\rho + b/\rho$ ‚Äî the $b/\rho$ term
  diverges as $\rho\to 0$, pulling the estimate **up**, away from noise-fitting
  (the practical default for weak identification).
- `halfnormal_prior(sd)`: $-\log p = \rho^2/(2\,\text{sd}^2)$ ‚Äî a soft cap pulling
  the estimate **down**, away from over-smooth near-linear fits.
- `lognormal_prior(mu, sd)`: $-\log p = (\log\rho-\mu)^2/(2\,\text{sd}^2)+\log\rho$
  ‚Äî a symmetric-in-$\log\rho$ pull toward a prior median $e^{\mu}$.

These are the lightweight, GPU-friendly stand-in for a full Bayesian prior on
$\rho$ ‚Äî see the aside in @sec-bayes-aside on the empirical-Bayes vs. full-Bayes
distinction.

## Multiple dimensions and ARD {#sec-nd}

For a spatial smooth ‚Äî thickness over a 2-D flattened patch, or a smooth
interaction of two covariates ‚Äî pass `x` as `(N, D)`. HSGP generalises by
**tensor product**: the eigenfunctions of the Laplacian on a box are products of
the 1-D standing waves, $\phi_{\mathbf j}(\mathbf x) = \prod_d \phi_{j_d}(x_d)$,
with frequency magnitude $\|\boldsymbol\omega_{\mathbf j}\| = \sqrt{\sum_d
\omega_{j_d}^2}$ (`_hsgp_eigen_nd`, `_hsgp_features_nd`). The kernel enters through
the same spectral density, now evaluated in $D$ dimensions
(`spectral_density(..., dim=D)`), and `gp_fit` defaults to `rank=8` *per axis*.
There are two ways to set lengthscales:

- **Isotropic** (`ard=False`, default): one shared lengthscale, a radial kernel of
  $\|\mathbf x-\mathbf x'\|$; the penalty uses the $D$-dimensional radial density
  $1/S_D(\|\boldsymbol\omega\|;\rho)$ (`_penalty_diag_nd_iso`).
- **ARD** (`ard=True`): **automatic relevance determination** ‚Äî a *separate*
  lengthscale $\rho_d$ per input dimension, so the fit can learn that thickness
  varies slowly along one axis and fast along another (and effectively switch an
  axis off by sending its $\rho_d$ large). The penalty factorises across axes,
  $1/\prod_d S_1(\omega_{j_d};\rho_d)$ (`_penalty_diag_nd_ard`); `gp_fit` estimates
  the $\rho_d$ by coordinate descent over the axes. `GPResult.nd_meta` records the
  per-axis ranks, bounds, and (for ARD) the per-axis lengthscales, so `gp_predict`
  can rebuild the tensor basis.

### Hierarchical GPs: `hgp_fit` {#sec-hgp}

When the data are grouped ‚Äî subjects, sites, sessions ‚Äî you may want a
**population** trajectory plus **per-group deviations** that share a kernel. That
is `hgp_fit`, the GP analogue of a random-slope mixed model and the "GS"
hierarchical GAM of Pedersen et al. (2019):

$$
y_{ij} = \beta_0 + f(x_{ij}) + f_{g(i)}(x_{ij}) + e_{ij},\quad
f\sim\mathcal{GP}(0,\sigma_{\mathrm{pop}}^2 K_\rho),\quad
f_g\sim\mathcal{GP}(0,\sigma_{\mathrm{grp}}^2 K_\rho)\ \text{iid}.
$$

Both the population and the group smooths use the *same* fixed HSGP eigenbasis
$\Phi$; stacking the population columns with the group columns ($\Phi$ masked to
each group's rows) gives one penalised design with a **block-diagonal, fully
diagonal** penalty ‚Äî one smoothing-parameter block per variance component,
$\operatorname{blkdiag}(\lambda_{\mathrm{pop}}\operatorname{diag}(1/S),\,
\lambda_{\mathrm{grp}}\operatorname{diag}(1/S)\otimes I_L)$. The two smoothing
parameters $\lambda_{\mathrm{pop}}=\sigma_e^2/\sigma_{\mathrm{pop}}^2$ and
$\lambda_{\mathrm{grp}}=\sigma_e^2/\sigma_{\mathrm{grp}}^2$ are selected by the
same Fellner‚ÄìSchall step as a multi-smooth GAM, and the shared $\rho$ by the same
pooled-REML grid as `gp_fit`. Because every block is diagonal with disjoint
columns, the FS penalty trace keeps the closed form $\operatorname{rank}_k/\lambda_k$
and there is still no eigendecomposition. The result is an `HGPResult` whose
`theta` carries $[\log\sigma_{\mathrm{pop}}^2,\log\sigma_{\mathrm{grp}}^2,\log\sigma_e^2,
\log\rho]$ (`model='GS'`), with a two-level `'nested'` variant for `(gp | g1/g2)`.
Partial pooling falls out for free: a group with few observations is shrunk toward
the population curve, exactly as a random effect is shrunk toward its mean.

## Aside: if you know GPs from Bayesian inference {#sec-bayes-aside}

You may have met Gaussian processes the *other* way round ‚Äî as Bayesian
nonparametric priors over functions, fit by maximising a marginal likelihood and
reported as a posterior mean with credible bands (Rasmussen & Williams). Nothing
here contradicts that; it is the **same object in the weight-space dialect**, and
the translation is exact.

A GP has two equivalent descriptions. The **function-space** view you know writes
a prior directly over functions, $f\sim\mathcal{GP}(0,k)$. The **weight-space**
view writes $f(x)=\sum_j \gamma_j\,\psi_j(x)$ with a Gaussian prior on the weights ‚Äî
which is exactly a basis expansion with a quadratic penalty. Mercer's theorem says
these are the same GP, and @eq-hsgp-kernel is precisely that bridge made finite:
the eigenfunctions are the features $\psi_j$ and the spectral density is the prior
variance. Reading this chapter's penalties as priors:

- the penalty matrix $S = K^{-1}$ **is the prior precision**;
- the penalised objective $\lVert\cdot\rVert_W^2 + \lambda\gamma^{\top}S\gamma$ is
  $-\log$ posterior, so the **penalised fit $\hat f$ is the GP posterior mean**
  (the MAP, which for a Gaussian *is* the mean);
- you still get uncertainty: the Bayesian coefficient covariance
  $V=(\Psi^{\top}W\Psi + S)^{-1}$ (`GPResult.cov_unscaled`) is the **(conditional)
  posterior covariance**, and the confidence bands of the
  [GAM chapter](gam.qmd#sec-edf) are the GP's posterior credible bands.

How the hyperparameters are chosen will also be familiar. Optimising the
lengthscale and amplitude by (RE)ML here is the same **marginal-likelihood /
evidence maximisation** ‚Äî "type-II MLE", "empirical Bayes" ‚Äî that GP toolkits
(GPML, scikit-learn, GPyTorch) use to set kernel hyperparameters; REML only adds a
correction for the degrees of freedom spent on the fixed effects $X$. And **HSGP
is not a `nitrix` invention**: the Laplace-eigenfunction-plus-spectral-density
construction is the reduced-rank Hilbert-space GP of Solin & S√§rkk√§ (2020), the
same approximation behind the `gp()` term in Stan/`brms` and PyMC's `HSGP` ‚Äî here
fit by penalised likelihood rather than MCMC.

| In Bayesian-GP language | In this suite |
|---|---|
| kernel / covariance $k$ | penalty $S = K^{-1}$ (a [random effect](lme.qmd)) |
| output scale / signal variance $\sigma_f^2$ | amplitude $=1/\lambda$ ([Fellner‚ÄìSchall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update) parameter) |
| lengthscale $\ell$ | lengthscale $\rho$ (`theta[:, 2]`) |
| observation noise / nugget $\sigma_n^2$ | residual variance $\sigma_e^2$ (`dispersion`) |
| log marginal likelihood / evidence | the (RE)ML objective (@eq-reml-nll; [LME](lme.qmd#sec-reml)) |
| type-II MLE / empirical Bayes | REML hyperparameter estimation |
| prior on the lengthscale | a `map_rho=` penalty ‚Üí MAP $\rho$ (@sec-priors) |
| GP posterior mean | the penalised fit $\hat f$ (`coef`) |
| GP posterior variance | $\operatorname{diag}(V)$ ‚Üí credible bands (`cov_unscaled`) |
| inducing points / sparse / reduced-rank | the $m$ retained eigenfunctions (`rank`) |
| ARD / per-dimension lengthscales | `ard=True` ($\rho_d$ per axis, @sec-nd) |
| sparse-spectrum / Hilbert-space features | the design $\Psi=[\sqrt{S(\omega_j)}\,\phi_j]$ |

The one honest difference: `nitrix` does **empirical-Bayes** GP regression, not
full Bayesian inference. It produces point estimates of $(\rho,\sigma_f^2,\sigma_e^2)$
by marginal likelihood and then a closed-form Gaussian posterior over $f$ given
those ‚Äî no MCMC, no posterior *over* the hyperparameters. A `map_rho=` prior adds a
MAP regulariser on $\rho$, but still selects a single value; it is not a posterior
over $\rho$. That is the deliberate trade for fitting hundreds of thousands of GPs
at once on a GPU; placing priors on $\rho$ and sampling them is out of scope here
(Stan/`brms`/PyMC territory).

## The ladder, complete

A Gaussian process turned out to be the final entry in the same list: a penalty
block, now a **kernel** block, whose own hyperparameters are estimable because
HSGP made them a diagonal reweighting. The whole suite remains the one sentence
from the [GAMM chapter](gamm.qmd#sec-unification):

> solve $(X^{\top}WX + \sum_k \lambda_k S_k)\gamma = X^{\top}Wz$ by penalised
> IRLS, and choose the $\lambda_k$ (and, for a kernel block, its lengthscale) by
> marginal likelihood.

From a straight line to a Gaussian process, it is one engine.

## Further reading

- Solin, A. & S√§rkk√§, S. (2020). *Hilbert space methods for reduced-rank Gaussian
  process regression.* Statistics and Computing 30, 419‚Äì446. ‚Äî The construction
  this chapter is built on: Laplace eigenfunctions reweighted by the spectral
  density.
- Riutort-Mayol, G., B√ºrkner, P.-C., Andersen, M. R., Solin, A. & Vehtari, A.
  (2023). *Practical Hilbert space approximate Bayesian Gaussian processes for
  probabilistic programming.* Statistics and Computing 33, 17. ‚Äî Practical
  guidance on choosing the rank $m$ and boundary factor $L$.
- Rasmussen, C. E. & Williams, C. K. I. (2006). *Gaussian Processes for Machine
  Learning.* MIT Press (free online). ‚Äî The function-space view, kernels, and
  evidence maximisation; the reference behind the Bayesian aside.
- Wood, S. N. & Fasiolo, M. (2017). *A generalized Fellner‚ÄìSchall method for
  smoothing parameter optimization.* Biometrics 73, 1071‚Äì1081. ‚Äî The inner
  $\lambda$ / amplitude step `gp_fit` reuses.
- Pedersen, E. J., Miller, D. L., Simpson, G. L. & Ross, N. (2019). *Hierarchical
  generalized additive models in ecology.* PeerJ 7, e6876. ‚Äî The "GS" hierarchical
  smoother behind `hgp_fit`.

## See also

- Code (ships with the `stats-gp` work):
  [`nitrix.stats.gp`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gp.py)
  (`gp_fit`, `gp_predict`, `GPResult`, `gp_aic`, `gp_bic`),
  [`nitrix.stats.hgp`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/hgp.py)
  (`hgp_fit`, `hgp_predict`, `HGPResult`),
  [`nitrix.stats.basis`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/basis.py)
  (`hsgp_basis`, `hsgp_basis_nd`, `gp_basis`, `gp_factor_smooth`),
  [`nitrix.stats.priors`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/priors.py)
  (`halfnormal_prior`, `invgamma_prior`, `lognormal_prior`),
  [`nitrix.linalg.kernel`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/kernel.py)
  (`spectral_density`).
- Rests on: [GAM](gam.qmd) (penalty blocks), [LME](lme.qmd) (the fixed-kernel
  view + the spectral trick), [Fellner‚ÄìSchall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update)
  (amplitude), [GAMM](gamm.qmd#sec-unification) (the unified list),
  [solvers](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky) (`small_inv_logdet`),
  [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence) (penalty-is-precision).
